summary: Gruber previews the upcoming 2008 Macworld Expo keynote by examining Apple's culture of secrecy and what it reveals about their announcements. He compares Apple punditry to Cold War Kremlinology, where silence and subtle signals matter more than official statements. He argues Apple engineers don't leak because they genuinely love their jobs and have no comparable alternative employer. While noting that Apple occasionally manages expectations behind the scenes when speculation runs too hot, he observes that this year's pre-Expo silence is notable but lacks the singular focus that preceded the iPhone announcement. He predicts a 'medium' keynote — interesting but not historic.
key_points: Apple's secrecy works because engineers love their jobs and have no comparable alternative employer, not because of draconian enforcement
When Apple goes silent both officially and privately in the face of intense speculation, that's the strongest signal they have something big
The massive free publicity from pre-keynote speculation can backfire if announcements don't meet inflated expectations
Apple quietly manages expectations behind the scenes when speculation outpaces what they plan to deliver
The early release of new Mac Pros the week before Macworld suggests a full keynote plate, but the lack of focused speculation points to a 'medium' event
money_quotes: One thing that makes being an Apple pundit fun is that it's akin to Cold-War era Kremlinology — to predict or analyze an opaque, secretive organization, you've got to read between the lines of the few things they do say, and you've got to know how to interpret silence.

Apple engineers really like their jobs. If you want to work for a company like Apple, on products like those that Apple makes, you've pretty much got to work for Apple, because there are no similar companies.
That's 200 engineers who knew specific details about the iPhone, not a single one of whom leaked to the press.
When, in the face of white-hot speculation, Apple goes totally silent both officially and privately, that's when they have something big.

While everyone marvels at the massive amount of 'free' publicity that Apple garners during the 'let's speculate about what they're going to announce' pre-keynote prelude, that publicity and speculation does Apple no good and even hurts them if what they're actually prepared to announce doesn't meet the level of speculation.

In a mild/medium/hot scale, where mild is a lame keynote that's mostly a 'state of the Apple Union' address and hot is a major new product along the lines of the iPhone, my gut feeling is that we're looking at a medium — spicy enough to be enjoyable, but not one for the ages.
key_insight: Apple's secrecy culture succeeds not through enforcement but because its engineers have no comparable employer, and the art of reading Apple lies in interpreting their silence rather than their statements.
